468	\begin{proof}
469	  \uses{def:cech_complex}
470	  Write \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and \(\mathcal{F}|_U = \widetilde{M}\) for an
471	  \(A\)-module \(M\). By Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex} the {\v C}ech complex of
472	  the standard cover is the complex of localisations
473	  \[
474	    \prod_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \;\to\;
475	    \prod_{i_0 i_1} M_{f_{i_0} f_{i_1}} \;\to\;
476	    \prod_{i_0 i_1 i_2} M_{f_{i_0} f_{i_1} f_{i_2}} \;\to\; \cdots,
477	  \]
478	  and the claim \(\check{H}^p = 0\) for \(p > 0\) is equivalent to exactness of the
479	  extended complex \(0 \to M \to \prod_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to \cdots\). Exactness may
480	  be checked after localising at an arbitrary prime \(\mathfrak{p} \subset A\).
481	  Choose an index \(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\) with
482	  \(f_{i_{\mathrm{fix}}} \notin \mathfrak{p}\); then
483	  \(M_{f_{i_{\mathrm{fix}}}, \mathfrak{p}} = M_{\mathfrak{p}}\), and the
484	  prescription \(h(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = s_{i_{\mathrm{fix}} i_0 \ldots i_p}\)
485	  defines a contracting homotopy of the localised extended complex: a direct
486	  computation gives \((dh + hd)(s) = s\), so the identity is homotopic to zero and
487	  the localised complex is exact. As exactness holds after every localisation, the
488	  extended complex is exact and \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) for
489	  \(p > 0\).
490	
491	  For the sheaf-cohomology statement, take the affine opens of \(U\) as a basis of
492	  the topology and the standard open covers as the admissible covers. Intersections
493	  of standard opens are again standard opens, standard covers are cofinal among
494	  covers of a basic open, and the {\v C}ech vanishing just proved supplies the
495	  acyclicity hypothesis; the {\v C}ech-to-cohomology comparison on a basis then
496	  yields \(H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) for all \(p > 0\). \emph{(This is Serre
497	  vanishing for quasi-coherent sheaves on affine schemes.)}
498	
499	  \emph{This proof depends on the following currently-absent Mathlib
500	  infrastructure: the explicit description of the standard-cover {\v C}ech complex
501	  as the complex of localisations \(\prod M_{f_{i_0}} \to \prod M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}}
502	  \to \cdots\) together with the prime-local, module-level contracting homotopy
503	  \(h(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = s_{i_{\mathrm{fix}} i_0 \ldots i_p}\) witnessing its
504	  positive-degree exactness, neither of which is available for sheaves of modules
505	  on a scheme.}
506	\end{proof}
507	
508	\section{The {\v C}ech complex computes $R^i f_*$}
509	
510	\begin{lemma}
511	
512	\leanok
513	[{\v C}ech complex computes the higher direct images]
514	  \label{lem:cech_computes_cohomology}
515	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cech_computes_higherDirectImage}
516	  \uses{lem:cech_acyclic_affine, def:cech_complex}
517	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Tag 02KE; Lemma
518	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent} ({\v C}ech computes cohomology when
519	  % all intersections are affine) and Lemma
520	  % \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application}
521	  % ($H^q(X,\mathcal{F}) = H^0(S, R^q f_*\mathcal{F})$ for affine $S$)
522	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L245--256 and L843--854).
523	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $X$ be a scheme.
524	  % Let $\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i$ be an open covering such that
525	  % $U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}$ is affine open for all $p \ge 0$ and all
526	  % $i_0, \ldots, i_p \in I$.
527	  % In this case for any quasi-coherent sheaf $\mathcal{F}$ we have
528	  % $$
529	  % \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = H^p(X, \mathcal{F})
530	  % $$
531	  % as $\Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$-modules for all $p$."
532	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
533	  % Assume that $f$ is quasi-separated and quasi-compact.
534	  % Assume $S$ is affine.
535	  % For any quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module $\mathcal{F}$
536	  % we have
537	  % $$
538	  % H^q(X, \mathcal{F}) = H^0(S, R^qf_*\mathcal{F})
539	  % $$
540	  % for all $q \in \mathbf{Z}$."
541	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Tag 02KE
542	  (\texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent}) and
543	  \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application}.}
544	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a separated quasi-compact morphism of schemes, let
545	  \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and let
546	  \(\mathfrak{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite affine open cover of
547	  \(X\) (so, by separatedness, every intersection \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is
548	  affine). Then the cohomology sheaves of the relative {\v C}ech complex
549	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) of
550	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex} compute the higher direct images: for every
551	  \(i \geq 0\) there is a canonical isomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules
552	  \[
553	    H^i\bigl(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\bigr)
554	      \;\cong\; R^i f_*\mathcal{F}.
555	  \]
556	  In particular, over an affine base \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\), taking global
557	  sections gives \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) = \check{H}^i(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F}) =
558	  H^0(S, R^i f_*\mathcal{F})\) as \(A\)-modules for all \(i\).
559	
560	  Two points about the precise form of the comparison should be noted. First, the
561	  derived-functor higher direct image \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) on the right-hand side
562	  is the one obtained from injective resolutions, which is available only when the
563	  category of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules has enough injectives; accordingly this
564	  comparison is asserted under the hypothesis that injective resolutions exist in
565	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\), and it compares the (always-defined) {\v C}ech higher
566	  direct image of Definition~\ref{def:cech_higher_direct_image} against the
567	  derived-functor one only in that case. Second, we assert the comparison in the
568	  weak form of the \emph{existence} of an isomorphism, i.e.\ as
569	  \(\bigl(H^i(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})) \cong
570	  R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr)\) without singling out a chosen natural isomorphism.
571	  This existence statement is all the downstream consumers (representability of the
572	  relative Picard functor, local triviality of the comparison) require: they use
573	  only that the unconditional {\v C}ech \(R^i f_*\) and the derived-functor
574	  \(R^i f_*\) agree as objects, never a specific identification, so committing to a
575	  canonical natural transformation would add coherence obligations that play no
576	  role in the applications.
577	\end{lemma}
578	
579	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "In view of
580	% Lemma \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero}
581	% this is a special case of
582	% Cohomology, Lemma
583	% \ref{cohomology-lemma-cech-spectral-sequence-application}."
584	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Consider the Leray spectral sequence
585	% $E_2^{p, q} = H^p(S, R^qf_*\mathcal{F})$
586	% converging to $H^{p + q}(X, \mathcal{F})$, see
587	% Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-Leray}.
588	% By Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images}
589	% we see that the sheaves $R^qf_*\mathcal{F}$ are quasi-coherent.
590	% By Lemma \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero}
591	% we see that $E_2^{p, q} = 0$ when $p > 0$.
592	% Hence the spectral sequence degenerates at $E_2$ and we win."
593	\begin{proof}
594	  \uses{lem:cech_acyclic_affine, def:cech_complex}
595	  The question is local on \(S\), and the formation of both sides commutes with
596	  restriction to opens of \(S\), so we may assume \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) is
597	  affine. Because \(f\) is separated and quasi-compact, each finite intersection
598	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is affine, and \(f\) restricted to it is an affine
599	  morphism; by the affine acyclicity of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} the
600	  higher direct images of \(\mathcal{F}\) over each such intersection vanish, so the
601	  terms of the relative {\v C}ech complex carry no hidden higher cohomology.
602	
603	  Consider the {\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral sequence of the cover
604	  \(\mathfrak{U}\), with \(E_2\)-page
605	  \(E_2^{p,q} = \check{H}^p(\mathfrak{U}, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F}))\)
606	  converging to \(H^{p+q}(X, \mathcal{F})\). Since all intersections are affine,
607	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} forces the presheaf cohomology
608	  \(\underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F})\) to vanish on the intersections for \(q > 0\), so
609	  the spectral sequence collapses to its row \(q = 0\): the {\v C}ech cohomology of
610	  \(\mathcal{F}\) for the cover \(\mathfrak{U}\) computes the sheaf cohomology,
611	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F}) = H^p(X, \mathcal{F})\) for all \(p\),
612	  and the same identification holds relatively, i.e.\ the cohomology sheaves of
613	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) compute the cohomology
614	  sheaves of \(Rf_*\mathcal{F}\).
615	
616	  It remains to identify these cohomology sheaves with \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\). Over
617	  the affine base \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\), the Leray spectral sequence
618	  \(E_2^{p,q} = H^p(S, R^q f_*\mathcal{F}) \Rightarrow H^{p+q}(X, \mathcal{F})\)
619	  has \(R^q f_*\mathcal{F}\) quasi-coherent, whence \(H^p(S, R^q f_*\mathcal{F}) = 0\)
620	  for \(p > 0\) by Serre vanishing (Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}). The Leray
621	  sequence therefore degenerates at \(E_2\), giving
622	  \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) = H^0(S, R^i f_*\mathcal{F})\). Combining with the previous
623	  paragraph, \(H^i\bigl(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\bigr)
624	  \cong R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) as \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules for all \(i \geq 0\).
625	
626	  \emph{This proof depends on the following currently-absent Mathlib
627	  infrastructure: the {\v C}ech-to-derived-functor spectral sequence and the Leray
628	  spectral sequence for the category of sheaves of modules on a scheme, neither of
629	  which is presently available there; the argument uses both to collapse onto the
630	  comparison isomorphism.}
631	\end{proof}
632	
633	\section{The unconditional higher direct image}
634	
635	\begin{definition}
636	
637	\leanok
638	[Unconditional higher direct image via {\v C}ech]
639	  \label{def:cech_higher_direct_image}
640	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cechHigherDirectImage}
641	  \uses{lem:cech_computes_cohomology}
642	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
643	  % \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application}
644	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L843--854); the unconditional
645	  % packaging via the {\v C}ech complex is an Archon-original construction grounded
646	  % in this lemma and Lemma \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent}.
647	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
648	  % Assume that $f$ is quasi-separated and quasi-compact.
649	  % Assume $S$ is affine.
650	  % For any quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module $\mathcal{F}$
651	  % we have
652	  % $$
653	  % H^q(X, \mathcal{F}) = H^0(S, R^qf_*\mathcal{F})
654	  % $$
655	  % for all $q \in \mathbf{Z}$."
656	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
657	  \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application}; unconditional
658	  {\v C}ech packaging is Archon-original.}
659	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a separated quasi-compact morphism and \(\mathcal{F}\) a
660	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. Fix a finite affine open cover
661	  \(\mathfrak{U}\) of \(X\). The \emph{(unconditional) \(i\)-th higher direct
662	  image} is defined, for each \(i \geq 0\), as the \(i\)-th cohomology sheaf of the
663	  relative {\v C}ech complex,
664	  \[
665	    R^i f_*\mathcal{F} \;:=\;
666	      H^i\bigl(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\bigr)
667	      \in \operatorname{QCoh}(S).
668	  \]
669	  This requires \emph{no} hypothesis that the category of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
670	  have enough injectives: the right-hand side is the cohomology of an explicit
671	  complex of quasi-coherent sheaves. By
672	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_computes_cohomology} the result is canonically isomorphic to
673	  the derived-functor higher direct image wherever the latter is defined, and in
674	  particular is independent of the chosen affine cover \(\mathfrak{U}\) up to
675	  canonical isomorphism: two affine covers admit a common affine refinement, and
676	  the induced refinement maps on {\v C}ech complexes are quasi-isomorphisms because
677	  both compute the same \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) by
678	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_computes_cohomology}. For \(i = 0\) one recovers the ordinary
679	  pushforward \(R^0 f_*\mathcal{F} = f_*\mathcal{F}\).
680	\end{definition}
681	
682	\section{Flat base change for the {\v C}ech higher direct images}
683	
684	\begin{lemma}
685	
686	\leanok
687	[Flat base change of $R^i f_*$ via {\v C}ech]
688	  \label{lem:cech_flat_base_change}
689	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cech_flatBaseChange}
690	  \uses{def:cech_higher_direct_image, lem:cech_computes_cohomology}
691	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Tag 02KH (Lemma
692	  % \texttt{lemma-flat-base-change-cohomology}, "Flat base change")
693	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L947--970).
694	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Consider a cartesian diagram of schemes
695	  % $$
696	  % \xymatrix{
697	  % X' \ar[d]_{f'} \ar[r]_{g'} & X \ar[d]^f \\
698	  % S' \ar[r]^g & S
699	  % }
700	  % $$
701	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
702	  % with pullback $\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}$.
703	  % Assume that $g$ is flat and that $f$ is quasi-compact and quasi-separated.
704	  % For any $i \geq 0$
705	  % \begin{enumerate}
706	  % \item the base change map of
707	  % Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-base-change-map-flat-case}
708	  % is an isomorphism
709	  % $$
710	  % g^*R^if_*\mathcal{F} \longrightarrow R^if'_*\mathcal{F}',
711	  % $$
712	  % \item if $S = \Spec(A)$ and $S' = \Spec(B)$, then
713	  % $H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B = H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')$.
714	  % \end{enumerate}"
715	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Tag 02KH
716	  (\texttt{lemma-flat-base-change-cohomology}, ``Flat base change'').}
717	  Consider the cartesian square
718	  \[
719	    \begin{array}{ccc}
720	      X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
721	      {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
722	      S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
723	    \end{array}
724	  \]
725	  with \(f : X \to S\) separated and quasi-compact, \(\mathcal{F}\) a
726	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and
727	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). Assume \(g\) is flat. Then for every
728	  \(i \geq 0\) the canonical base-change map between the unconditional {\v C}ech
729	  higher direct images of Definition~\ref{def:cech_higher_direct_image} is an
730	  isomorphism
731	  \[
732	    g^*\bigl(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
733	      R^i f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr) = R^i f'_*\mathcal{F}'.
734	  \]
735	  Equivalently, when \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and
736	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with \(A \to B\) flat, the comparison map of
737	  \(B\)-modules \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \to H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')\) is an
738	  isomorphism.
739	\end{lemma}
740	
741	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Having said this, part (1) follows from part (2). Namely,
742	% part (1) is local on $S'$ and hence we may assume $S$
743	% and $S'$ are affine. ...
744	% In case $X$ is separated, choose an affine open covering
745	% $\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \ldots \cup U_t$ and recall that
746	% $$
747	% \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = H^p(X, \mathcal{F}),
748	% $$
749	% see
750	% Lemma \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent}.
751	% If $\mathcal{U}_B : X_B = (U_1)_B \cup \ldots \cup (U_t)_B$ we obtain
752	% by base change, then it is still the case that each $(U_i)_B$ is affine
753	% and that $X_B$ is separated. ...
754	% We have the following relation between the {\v C}ech complexes
755	% $$
756	% \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B) =
757	% \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B
758	% $$
759	% as follows from
760	% Lemma \ref{lemma-affine-base-change}.
761	% Since $A \to B$ is flat, the same thing remains true on taking cohomology."
762	\begin{proof}
763	  \uses{def:cech_higher_direct_image, lem:cech_computes_cohomology}
764	  The assertion that the base-change map is an isomorphism is local on \(S'\), so we
765	  may assume \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with
766	  \(A \to B\) flat. By Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_computes_cohomology} both
767	  \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) and \(R^i f'_*\mathcal{F}'\) are quasi-coherent, associated
768	  to the \(A\)-module \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F})\) and the \(B\)-module
769	  \(H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')\) respectively; since pullback along \(g\) corresponds to
770	  \(- \otimes_A B\) on modules, it suffices to prove that the comparison map
771	  \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \to H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')\) is an isomorphism.
772	
773	  Choose a finite affine open cover \(\mathfrak{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\);
774	  by separatedness all intersections \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) are affine. Base
775	  changing the cover yields an affine open cover \(\mathfrak{U}_B\) of the
776	  again-separated \(X' = X_B\), with each \((U_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_B\) affine. By
777	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_computes_cohomology} the two {\v C}ech complexes compute the
778	  respective cohomologies. The affine base change for the \(i = 0\) direct image
779	  identifies each term of the base-changed {\v C}ech complex with the original term
780	  tensored over \(A\) with \(B\), giving an isomorphism of cochain complexes
781	  \[
782	    \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B)
783	      \;\cong\;
784	      \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B.
785	  \]
786	  Because \(A \to B\) is flat, \(- \otimes_A B\) is exact and commutes with the
787	  formation of cohomology of a complex; applying \(H^i\) for any \(i \geq 0\) yields
788	  \[
789	    H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
790	      H^i(X', \mathcal{F}').
791	  \]
792	  Translating back to sheaves on \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) — using that the
793	  unconditional higher direct images are the quasi-coherent sheaves associated to
794	  these modules — gives the asserted isomorphism
795	  \(g^*(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}) \xrightarrow{\sim} R^i f'_*\mathcal{F}'\) for all
796	  \(i \geq 0\). The flatness of \(g\) (hence of \(g'\), by base change) is exactly
797	  what makes \(- \otimes_A B\) exact, and is therefore the precise hypothesis under
798	  which the {\v C}ech computation of Definition~\ref{def:cech_higher_direct_image}
799	  is compatible with base change.
800	
801	  \emph{This proof depends on the following currently-absent Mathlib
802	  infrastructure: the term-wise affine base change of the {\v C}ech complex
803	  identifying \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B) \cong
804	  \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B\), and the
805	  exactness of \(- \otimes_A B\) (its commutation with the formation of cohomology
806	  of a complex) for the relevant categories of sheaves of modules, neither of which
807	  is presently available there.}
808	\end{proof}
809

# Analogy: Does Mathlib's pushforward/pullback pseudofunctor coherence close the D3′ Sq1 `sheafificationCompPullback` composition tail?

## Mode
api-alignment

## Slug
ma-d3264

## Iteration
264

## Question
Does the Mathlib `Pseudofunctor (LocallyDiscrete Schemeᵒᵖ) (Adj Cat)` coherence (the engine's
`conjugateEquiv_*` / `pseudofunctor_*` lemmas) apply to the D3′ Sq1 tail
(`sheafificationCompPullback_comp_tail`), whose units are units of the COMPOSITE adjunction
`B_φ := (PresheafOfModules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction φ').comp sheafificationAdjunction`,
absorbing the extra sheafification layer automatically? If not, is the project carrying a parallel
API, and what is the concrete next-step lemma set?

## Project artifact(s)
- `AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean:2475` — `sheafificationCompPullback_comp_tail` (the open sorry).
- `AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean:2532` — `sheafificationCompPullback_comp` (caller; transpose + R0-peel already done).
- `AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean:2443` — `sheaf_unit_comp_pushforward_pullbackComp_inv` (R0-peel building block, axiom-clean).
- `AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean:874` — `unitToPushforwardObjUnit_comp` (bare-adjunction analog, `rfl`).
- `AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean:915` — `pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp` (the bare-adjunction MODEL the tail mirrors, L920–1001).
- `AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/CechHigherDirectImage.lean:175` — `pushPullMap` + deferred functor-law comment block (L189–212).

## Decisions identified

### Decision 1 — Do the sheaf-level pseudofunctor coherences absorb the sheafification layer of the D3′ tail?

- **Mathlib idiom**: `Scheme.Modules.pullback`/`pushforward`/`pullbackComp`/`pushforwardComp`/`pullbackId`/`pushforwardId` ARE the 1-/2-cells of `AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.pseudofunctor : Pseudofunctor (LocallyDiscrete Scheme.{u}ᵒᵖ) (Adj Cat)` (`Mathlib/AlgebraicGeometry/Modules/Sheaf.lean:300`). The coherences `pseudofunctor_associativity`/`_left_unitality`/`_right_unitality` (`Sheaf.lean:246,263,277`) and `conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv` (`Sheaf.lean:238`) are statements **purely about these sheaf-level isos**. At the sheaf level the pushforward coherence is sectionwise trivial: `pushforwardComp_hom_app_app : ((pushforwardComp f g).hom.app M).app U = 𝟙 _` is `rfl` (`Sheaf.lean:214`).
- **Project's path**: The engine's `pushPullMap` (`CechHigherDirectImage.lean:175`) lives ENTIRELY at the sheaf level — it only manipulates `Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp`/`pushforwardComp`/`pullbackId`/`pushforwardId`. Its deferred functor laws `pushPullMap_id`/`pushPullMap_comp` are therefore exactly what the pseudofunctor coherences govern, and the engine's comment (L205) is correct that they are "already in Mathlib". The D3′ tail is DIFFERENT: its subject is `SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback` (`Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Sheaf/PullbackContinuous.lean:117`), the comparison iso `sheafification ⋙ SheafOfModules.pullback ≅ PresheafOfModules.pullback ⋙ sheafification` — i.e. the bridge BETWEEN the sheaf pseudofunctor and the presheaf-pullback-then-sheafify pseudofunctor. The "extra sheafification layer" is *exactly* what `sheafificationCompPullback` IS.
- **Gap**: divergent-and-the-engine-route-does-not-transfer. The pseudofunctor coherences never mention `sheafificationCompPullback`; they live one level ABOVE it. Mathlib has NO composition / naturality / coherence lemma for `sheafificationCompPullback` — the global grep returns only its `def` at `PullbackContinuous.lean:117`. The sheaf-level coherences cannot, by themselves, say how `sheafificationCompPullback` composes across `h ≫ f`.
- **Cost of divergence**: none — there is no shipped divergent code; this is a "can the cheap route be reused?" question, and the answer is no.
- **Verdict**: NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL (the composition coherence of `sheafificationCompPullback` is genuinely Mathlib-absent). The engine route does NOT close the D3′ tail.

### Decision 2 — Is the project carrying a parallel API (`PrPbPushAdj` / `sheafAdj` / `sheafificationCompPullback`)?

- **Mathlib idiom**: `SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback` is Mathlib's own def, built as `Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq` of two COMPOSITE adjunctions (`PullbackContinuous.lean:117–125`): `B := (sheafificationAdjunction (𝟙 S.obj)).comp (pullbackPushforwardAdjunction φ)` and `A := (PresheafOfModules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction φ.hom).comp (sheafificationAdjunction (𝟙 R.obj))`. The relevant `leftAdjointUniq` API is in `Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Adjunction/Unique.lean` (`homEquiv_leftAdjointUniq_hom_app:39`, `unit_leftAdjointUniq_hom_app:51`, `leftAdjointUniq_trans:79`).
- **Project's path**: `PrPbPushAdj`/`sheafAdj` are **comment mnemonics only** for `PresheafOfModules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction`/`sheafificationAdjunction` (both Mathlib); `B_φ` is a genuine `Adjunction.comp` of Mathlib adjunctions — exactly the decomposition Mathlib's `sheafificationCompPullback` def uses; `sheafificationCompPullback_eq_leftAdjointUniq` (`TensorObjSubstrate.lean:1603`) is a `rfl`-bridge to the Mathlib def. The transpose route in `sheafificationCompPullback_comp` (`apply …homEquiv.injective; rw [sheafificationCompPullback_eq_leftAdjointUniq]; erw [homEquiv_leftAdjointUniq_hom_app]`) is the correct Mathlib idiom for a `leftAdjointUniq` identity.
- **Gap**: identical — no parallel API. The project consumes Mathlib's `sheafificationCompPullback` and the Mathlib `leftAdjointUniq` mate API directly.
- **Cost of divergence**: none.
- **Verdict**: PROCEED — no parallel API; do NOT refactor `B_φ`/`sheafCompPb` into pseudofunctor 1-cells (there is nothing to re-express; the pseudofunctor 1-cells are a strictly higher layer that cannot reach this comparison).

#### Why `leftAdjointUniq_trans` does NOT shortcut it
`Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq_trans` (`Unique.lean:79`) composes `leftAdjointUniq` isos sharing the **same** right adjoint `G`. The three `sheafificationCompPullback` isos here (for `f`, `h`, `h ≫ f`) have **different** right adjoints (`pushforward f`, `pushforward h`, `pushforward (h≫f)`), and the comparison is **conjugated** by `pullbackComp`/`pushforwardComp`. The conjugated-across-varying-right-adjoint version is precisely the absent gap. Mathlib's closest precedent is `pullbackComp = Adjunction.leftAdjointCompIso` with `Adjunction.conjugateEquiv_leftAdjointCompIso_inv` (`Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Adjunction/CompositionIso.lean:82`) — but that is for `leftAdjointCompIso`, not for the `leftAdjointUniq` bridge to a parallel pseudofunctor.

## Recommendation

We are in WORLD 2: the engine's Mathlib-pseudofunctor route does NOT apply to the D3′ tail, because the
tail's subject `sheafificationCompPullback` is the sheafification-bridge that lives one level *below*
the pseudofunctor coherences — and Mathlib supplies no composition coherence for it. The project is
**not** carrying a parallel API; it is already consuming the right Mathlib objects (`sheafificationCompPullback`,
the composite `Adjunction.comp`, `homEquiv_leftAdjointUniq_hom_app`). So the documented ~50–80 LOC
project-local mate calculus IS the correct route — there is no cheap substitute. Dispatch the prover to
complete it by **mirroring `pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp` L952–1001 one composite-adjunction level up**.

### Concrete next-step lemma set for `sheafificationCompPullback_comp_tail`
The R0-peel is done (`sheaf_unit_comp_pushforward_pullbackComp_inv`, via `unit_conjugateEquiv` +
`Scheme.Modules.conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv`). The remaining R1/R5 collapse mirrors the model:

1. `Adjunction.homEquiv_leftAdjointUniq_hom_app` (Mathlib `Unique.lean:39`) on each
   `(sheafificationCompPullback f/h).app _ .hom` (via `sheafificationCompPullback_eq_leftAdjointUniq`)
   to recover R1 = `(pullback h).map (sheafCompPb f .app P).hom` and
   R5 = `(sheafCompPb h .app (PrPb_f P)).hom` as the composite-adjunction units `B_f.unit`/`B_h.unit`.
   This replaces the model's `hLf`/`hLh` (L942–951) — at composite level the units are NOT `rfl`,
   but `homEquiv_leftAdjointUniq_hom_app` delivers them directly.
2. `Adjunction.comp_unit_app` (expand each composite-adjunction unit as `sheafAdj.unit ≫ (forget⋙restr).map (prPbPush.unit)`) — replaces the model's `unitToPushforwardObjUnit_comp` `rfl` (the composite analog is the `comp_unit_app` expansion, applied to LHS `B_{h≫f}.unit` and reassembled).
3. `Adjunction.homEquiv_unit` + `Adjunction.homEquiv_naturality_left` / `_right` — the `hinner`/`hcomp'`/`key2` chain of the model (L960–994), one sheafification layer up.
4. `(Scheme.Modules.pushforwardComp h f).hom.naturality` — slide the sheaf `pushforwardComp.hom` past the recovered R1/R5 factors (model L997).
5. `Adjunction.unit_naturality` (+ `Functor.comp_map`, `← Functor.map_comp`/`Functor.map_comp`, `erw [Category.assoc]`) — the final collapse to `B_{h≫f}.unit` (model L988–1001).
6. For the presheaf δ_pre = `PresheafOfModules.pullbackComp φ'_f φ'_h .hom.app P` (a `leftAdjointCompIso`):
   transpose via `Adjunction.conjugateEquiv_leftAdjointCompIso_inv` (`CompositionIso.lean:82`) — there is
   no project-named `conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv` at the presheaf level, so reach for the abstract
   `leftAdjointCompIso` lemma directly.

Use `erw` (not `rw`) for the `Category.assoc`/`Functor.map_comp`/coherence steps — the `SheafOfModules`
compositions appear in defeq-but-not-syntactic forms, exactly as the model's docstring warns (L911–914).

variable (X) in
/-- The pushforward of sheaves of modules by the identity morphism identifies
to the identity functor. -/
def pushforwardId : pushforward (𝟙 X) ≅ 𝟭 _ :=
  SheafOfModules.pushforwardId _

@[simp] lemma pushforwardId_hom_app_app : ((pushforwardId X).hom.app M).app U = 𝟙 _ := rfl
@[simp] lemma pushforwardId_inv_app_app : ((pushforwardId X).inv.app M).app U = 𝟙 _ := rfl

variable (X) in
/-- The pullback of sheaves of modules by the identity morphism identifies
to the identity functor. -/
def pullbackId : pullback (𝟙 X) ≅ 𝟭 _ :=
  SheafOfModules.pullbackId _

variable (X) in
lemma conjugateEquiv_pullbackId_hom :
    conjugateEquiv .id (pullbackPushforwardAdjunction (𝟙 X)) (pullbackId X).hom =
      (pushforwardId X).inv :=
  SheafOfModules.conjugateEquiv_pullbackId_hom _

/-- The composition of two pushforward functors for sheaves of modules on schemes
identify to the pushforward for the composition. -/
def pushforwardComp :
    pushforward f ⋙ pushforward g ≅ pushforward (f ≫ g) :=
  SheafOfModules.pushforwardComp _ _

@[simp] lemma pushforwardComp_hom_app_app (U) : ((pushforwardComp f g).hom.app M).app U = 𝟙 _ := rfl
@[simp] lemma pushforwardComp_inv_app_app (U) : ((pushforwardComp f g).inv.app M).app U = 𝟙 _ := rfl

/-- The composition of two pullback functors for sheaves of modules on schemes
identify to the pullback for the composition. -/
def pullbackComp :
    pullback g ⋙ pullback f ≅ pullback (f ≫ g) :=
  SheafOfModules.pullbackComp _ _

/-- Pushforwards along equal morphisms are isomorphic. -/
def pushforwardCongr {f g : X ⟶ Y} (hf : f = g) : pushforward f ≅ pushforward g :=
    pushforwardNatIso _ (Opens.mapIso _ _ (hf ▸ rfl)) ≪≫
      SheafOfModules.pushforwardCongr (by cat_disch)

@[simp] lemma pushforwardCongr_hom_app_app {f g : X ⟶ Y} (hf : f = g) (U : Y.Opens) :
    ((pushforwardCongr hf).hom.app M).app U = M.presheaf.map (eqToHom (hf ▸ rfl)).op := rfl

@[simp] lemma pushforwardCongr_inv_app_app {f g : X ⟶ Y} (hf : f = g) (U : Y.Opens) :
    ((pushforwardCongr hf).inv.app M).app U = M.presheaf.map (eqToHom (hf ▸ rfl)).op := rfl

/-- Inverse images along equal morphisms are isomorphic. -/
def pullbackCongr {f g : X ⟶ Y} (hf : f = g) : pullback f ≅ pullback g :=
  eqToIso (hf ▸ rfl)

lemma conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv :
    conjugateEquiv ((pullbackPushforwardAdjunction g).comp (pullbackPushforwardAdjunction f))
      (pullbackPushforwardAdjunction (f ≫ g)) (pullbackComp f g).inv =
    (pushforwardComp f g).hom :=
  SheafOfModules.conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv _ _

`pullbackPushforwardAdjunction (h≫f)`, the left side becomes `unitToPushforwardObjUnit (h≫f)`
and the right side is reassembled via the conjugate/mate identity
`conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv` (relating `pullbackComp.inv` to `pushforwardComp.hom`),
`unit_conjugateEquiv`, and the composite-adjunction unit `Adjunction.comp_unit_app`.

Consumed by `pullbackUnitIso`: on an affine chart `V` the inclusion `V.ι ≫ f` factors as
`g ≫ U.ι` with `Opens.map g.base` `Final`, so two applications of this coherence (one for
each factorisation) express the restricted global comparison as a composite of isomorphisms
(`pbu` for an open immersion / a `Final`-chart morphism is an iso), whence
`pullbackObjUnitToUnit f` is an iso by `isIso_of_isIso_restrict`.

The proof uses `erw` for the associativity / functoriality steps because the `SheafOfModules`
category compositions appear in defeq-but-not-syntactic forms (`Scheme.Modules.pullback f`
vs `SheafOfModules.pullback f.toRingCatSheafHom`) on which plain `rw [Category.assoc]` /
`rw [Functor.map_comp]` fail to unify. -/
lemma pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp {X Y Z : Scheme.{u}} (h : Z ⟶ Y) (f : Y ⟶ X) :
    SheafOfModules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit (h ≫ f).toRingCatSheafHom =
      (Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp h f).inv.app (SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf) ≫
      (Scheme.Modules.pullback h).map (SheafOfModules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit f.toRingCatSheafHom) ≫
      SheafOfModules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit h.toRingCatSheafHom := by
  have key := unitToPushforwardObjUnit_comp h f
  have conj := unit_conjugateEquiv
    ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction f).comp
      (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction h))
    (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction (h ≫ f))
    (Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp h f).inv (SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf)
  rw [Scheme.Modules.conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv] at conj
  apply (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction (h ≫ f)).homEquiv _ _ |>.injective
  have hL : (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction (h ≫ f)).homEquiv _ _
      (SheafOfModules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit (h ≫ f).toRingCatSheafHom)
    = SheafOfModules.unitToPushforwardObjUnit (h ≫ f).toRingCatSheafHom := by
    exact SheafOfModules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction_homEquiv_pullbackObjUnitToUnit
      (h ≫ f).toRingCatSheafHom
  have hi : (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction (h ≫ f)).homEquiv _ _
      ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp h f).inv.app (SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf))
    = ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction f).comp
          (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction h)).unit.app
          (SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf) ≫
        (Scheme.Modules.pushforwardComp h f).hom.app
          ((Scheme.Modules.pullback f ⋙ Scheme.Modules.pullback h).obj
            (SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf)) := by
    rw [Adjunction.homEquiv_unit]; exact conj.symm
  have hLf : (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction f).homEquiv _ _
      (SheafOfModules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit f.toRingCatSheafHom)
    = SheafOfModules.unitToPushforwardObjUnit f.toRingCatSheafHom := by
    exact SheafOfModules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction_homEquiv_pullbackObjUnitToUnit
      f.toRingCatSheafHom
  have hLh : (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction h).homEquiv _ _
      (SheafOfModules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit h.toRingCatSheafHom)
    = SheafOfModules.unitToPushforwardObjUnit h.toRingCatSheafHom := by
    exact SheafOfModules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction_homEquiv_pullbackObjUnitToUnit
      h.toRingCatSheafHom
  have hinner : (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction h).unit.app
        ((Scheme.Modules.pullback f).obj (SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf)) ≫
      (Scheme.Modules.pushforward h).map
        ((Scheme.Modules.pullback h).map
            (SheafOfModules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit f.toRingCatSheafHom) ≫
          SheafOfModules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit h.toRingCatSheafHom)
    = SheafOfModules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit f.toRingCatSheafHom ≫
        SheafOfModules.unitToPushforwardObjUnit h.toRingCatSheafHom := by
    have e := Adjunction.homEquiv_unit (adj := Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction h)
      (X := (Scheme.Modules.pullback f).obj (SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf))
      (Y := SheafOfModules.unit Z.ringCatSheaf)
      (f := (Scheme.Modules.pullback h).map
          (SheafOfModules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit f.toRingCatSheafHom) ≫
        SheafOfModules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit h.toRingCatSheafHom)
    have key2 : (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction h).homEquiv _ _
          ((Scheme.Modules.pullback h).map
              (SheafOfModules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit f.toRingCatSheafHom) ≫
            SheafOfModules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit h.toRingCatSheafHom)
        = SheafOfModules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit f.toRingCatSheafHom ≫
            SheafOfModules.unitToPushforwardObjUnit h.toRingCatSheafHom := by
      rw [Adjunction.homEquiv_naturality_left]; exact congrArg _ hLh
    exact e.symm.trans key2
  have hcomp' : ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction f).comp
        (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction h)).unit.app
        (SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf) ≫
      (Scheme.Modules.pushforward h ⋙ Scheme.Modules.pushforward f).map
        ((Scheme.Modules.pullback h).map
            (SheafOfModules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit f.toRingCatSheafHom) ≫
          SheafOfModules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit h.toRingCatSheafHom)
    = SheafOfModules.unitToPushforwardObjUnit f.toRingCatSheafHom ≫
        (Scheme.Modules.pushforward f).map
          (SheafOfModules.unitToPushforwardObjUnit h.toRingCatSheafHom) := by
    have ef := Adjunction.homEquiv_unit (adj := Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction f)
      (X := SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf) (Y := SheafOfModules.unit Y.ringCatSheaf)
      (f := SheafOfModules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit f.toRingCatSheafHom)
    rw [Adjunction.comp_unit_app, Functor.comp_map]
    erw [Category.assoc, ← Functor.map_comp, hinner, Functor.map_comp]
    erw [← Category.assoc]
    rw [show (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction f).unit.app
            (SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf) ≫
          (Scheme.Modules.pushforward f).map
            (SheafOfModules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit f.toRingCatSheafHom)
        = SheafOfModules.unitToPushforwardObjUnit f.toRingCatSheafHom from ef.symm.trans hLf]
    rfl
  rw [hL, key, Adjunction.homEquiv_naturality_right, hi]
  erw [Category.assoc, ← (Scheme.Modules.pushforwardComp h f).hom.naturality
      ((Scheme.Modules.pullback h).map (SheafOfModules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit f.toRingCatSheafHom) ≫
        SheafOfModules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit h.toRingCatSheafHom)]
  erw [← Category.assoc, hcomp']
  erw [Category.assoc]

1:section conjugateEquiv
3:variable {C : Type u₁} {D : Type u₂}
4:variable [Category.{v₁} C] [Category.{v₂} D]
5:variable {L₁ L₂ : C ⥤ D} {R₁ R₂ : D ⥤ C}
6:variable (adj₁ : L₁ ⊣ R₁) (adj₂ : L₂ ⊣ R₂)
11:composition with the unitors. Corresponding natural transformations are called `conjugateEquiv`.
19:def conjugateEquiv : (L₂ ⟶ L₁) ≃ (R₁ ⟶ R₂) :=
29:theorem conjugateEquiv_counit (α : L₂ ⟶ L₁) (d : D) :
30:    L₂.map ((conjugateEquiv adj₁ adj₂ α).app _) ≫ adj₂.counit.app d =
35:theorem conjugateEquiv_counit_symm (α : R₁ ⟶ R₂) (d : D) :
37:      ((conjugateEquiv adj₁ adj₂).symm α).app _ ≫ adj₁.counit.app d := by
38:    conv_lhs => rw [← (conjugateEquiv adj₁ adj₂).right_inv α]
39:    exact (conjugateEquiv_counit adj₁ adj₂ ((conjugateEquiv adj₁ adj₂).symm α) d)
42:theorem unit_conjugateEquiv (α : L₂ ⟶ L₁) (c : C) :
43:    adj₁.unit.app _ ≫ (conjugateEquiv adj₁ adj₂ α).app _ =
45:  dsimp [conjugateEquiv]

lemma pushPullMap_id (F : X.Modules) (Y : Over X) :
    pushPullMap F (𝟙 Y) = 𝟙 (pushPullObj F Y) := by
  -- `star`: the unit-triangle for the identity adjunction `pullback 𝟙 ⊣ pushforward 𝟙`.
  have star := unit_conjugateEquiv (Adjunction.id (C := Y.left.Modules))
    (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction (𝟙 Y.left))
    (Scheme.Modules.pullbackId Y.left).hom ((Scheme.Modules.pullback Y.hom).obj F)
  rw [Scheme.Modules.conjugateEquiv_pullbackId_hom] at star
  simp only [Adjunction.id_unit, NatTrans.id_app, Category.id_comp] at star
  -- `hru`: right-unitality of the pullback pseudofunctor, applied at `F`.
  have hru := Scheme.Modules.pseudofunctor_right_unitality (X := Y.left) (f := Y.hom)
  have hru2 := congrArg (fun t => NatTrans.app t F) hru
  simp only [NatTrans.comp_app, Functor.whiskerLeft_app, Functor.rightUnitor_hom_app,
    Category.comp_id] at hru2
  sorry

input.new_string:
apply Scheme.Modules.hom_ext
    intro U; rfl
  -- clean `star` to `(pushforwardId).inv.app M = η ≫ (pushforward 𝟙).map (pullbackId.hom)`
  simp only [Functor.id_obj, Category.id_comp] at star
  rw [Category.comp_id] at hru2
  -- the unit zig-zag for the identity adjunction collapses on `M`
  have hzig : (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction (𝟙 Y.left)).unit.app
        ((Scheme.Modules.pullback Y.hom).obj F) ≫
      (Scheme.Modules.pushforwardId Y.left).hom.app
        ((Scheme.Modules.pullback (𝟙 Y.left)).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback Y.hom).obj F)) ≫
      (Scheme.Modules.pullbackId Y.left).hom.app ((Scheme.Modules.pullback Y.hom).obj F) =
      𝟙 ((Scheme.Modules.pullback Y.hom).obj F) := by
    have hnat := (Scheme.Modules.pushforwardId Y.left).hom.naturality
      ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackId Y.left).hom.app ((Scheme.Modules.pullback Y.hom).obj F))
    simp only [Functor.id_map] at hnat
    rw [← hnat, ← Category.assoc, ← star, Iso.inv_hom_id_app]
  -- the pullback comparison + the over-triangle transport collapse via right-unitality
  have hib_inner : (Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp (𝟙 Y.left) Y.hom).hom.app F ≫
      eqToHom (congrArg (fun q => (Scheme.Modules.pullback q).obj F) (Category.id_comp Y.hom)) =
      (Scheme.Modules.pullbackId Y.left).hom.app ((Scheme.Modules.pullback Y.hom).obj F) := by
    rw [eqToHom_app] at hru2
    rw [← hru2, ← Category.assoc, Iso.hom_inv_id_app, Category.id_comp]
  have hib : (Scheme.Modules.pushforward Y.hom).map
        ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp (𝟙 Y.left) Y.hom).hom.app F) ≫
      eqToHom (congrArg (fun q => (Scheme.Modules.pushforward Y.hom).obj
        ((Scheme.Modules.pullback q).obj F)) (Category.id_comp Y.hom)) =
      (Scheme.Modules.pushforward Y.hom).map
        ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackId Y.left).hom.app ((Scheme.Modules.pullback Y.hom).obj F)) := by
    rw [← eqToHom_map, ← Functor.map_comp, hib_inner]
  -- assemble
  simp only [pushPullMap, Over.id_left]
  rw [reassoc_of% hpf, hib, ← Functor.map_comp, ← Functor.map_comp, hzig,
    CategoryTheory.Functor.map_id]

# AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/CechHigherDirectImage.lean

## Summary
- **Declarations added: 1 axiom-clean** — `AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_id` (line ~216),
  `#print axioms` = `{propext, Classical.choice, Quot.sound}`, full `lake env lean` build EXIT=0.
- **Declarations blocked: 1** — `pushPullMap_comp` (the pentagon law); NOT added (no `sorry` permitted),
  documented in-file with the precise route.
- **sorry count: 4 → 4** (unchanged; all 4 are pre-existing infra-gated sorries: `CechNerve` L89,
  `CechAcyclic.affine`, `cech_computes_higherDirectImage`, `cech_flatBaseChange`). The `pushPullMap_id`
  lemma I added is sorry-free; I did NOT introduce any new sorry.

## pushPullMap_id (RESOLVED — axiom-clean)
- **Statement:** `pushPullMap F (𝟙 Y) = 𝟙 (pushPullObj F Y)` — the identity functor law of the
  push–pull functor `G`, the engine's dominant-pole deliverable.
- **Approach:** adjunction-mate calculus (the planner's recipe), built bottom-up:
  - `star := unit_conjugateEquiv (Adjunction.id) (pullbackPushforwardAdjunction (𝟙 Y.left)) (pullbackId Y.left).hom _`
    then `rw [conjugateEquiv_pullbackId_hom]` ⇒ the identity-adjunction unit triangle
    `(pushforwardId).inv.app M = η ≫ (pushforward 𝟙).map ((pullbackId).hom.app M)`.
  - `hru := pseudofunctor_right_unitality (X := Y.left) (f := Y.hom)`, applied at `F` ⇒ `hru2`
    (right-unitality of the pullback comparison; `(pullbackComp 𝟙 p).inv.app F ≫ (pullbackId).hom.app M = eqToHom`).
  - `hpf` — the pushforward-side coercion `pushforwardComp.hom.app ≫ eqToHom` collapses to
    `(pushforward p).map ((pushforwardId).hom.app N)`; proven by **`apply Scheme.Modules.hom_ext; intro U; rfl`**
    (sectionwise trivial because `pushforwardComp_hom_app_app`/`pushforwardId_hom_app_app` are `rfl`).
  - `hzig` — the unit zig-zag `η ≫ (pushforwardId).hom.app N ≫ (pullbackId).hom.app M = 𝟙 M`, via
    `(pushforwardId).hom.naturality` + `star` + `Iso.inv_hom_id_app`.
  - `hib_inner`/`hib` — the pullback comparison + over-triangle transport collapse to
    `(pushforward p).map ((pullbackId).hom.app M)` via `hru2` (`Iso.hom_inv_id_app`) and `eqToHom_map`.
  - assembly: `simp only [pushPullMap, Over.id_left]; erw [reassoc_of% hpf, hib, ← Functor.map_comp];
    erw [hzig, Functor.map_id]; rfl`.
- **Result:** RESOLVED — axiom-clean.

### Recipes / gotchas that made it work (reusable for `pushPullMap_comp`)
- **`pushforwardComp`/`pushforwardId` coercions are sectionwise `rfl`** ⇒ any
  `pushforwardComp.hom.app ≫ eqToHom = (pushforward _).map (…)` collapse is `Scheme.Modules.hom_ext; intro U; rfl`.
- **`pullbackId`/`pushforwardId` introduce `(𝟭 _).obj M` in cod/dom** that block syntactic `rw`
  (`Category.id_comp`/`comp_id`/`Iso.inv_hom_id_app` all fail). Fix: **use `erw`** (bridges the `(𝟭).obj M`
  vs `M` defeq) and **`reassoc_of%`** to match across associativity (`← Category.assoc` mysteriously
  fails to fire; `← reassoc_of% star` via `erw` succeeds).
- **eqToHom proof mismatch** (`Over.w (𝟙 Y)` in the goal vs `Category.id_comp Y.hom` in the helpers):
  state helpers with `Category.id_comp` and rewrite with **`erw`** (proof irrelevance is defeq).
- `unit_conjugateEquiv` is top-level `CategoryTheory.unit_conjugateEquiv` (NOT `Adjunction.…`).
- `eqToHom_map (F) h : F.map (eqToHom h) = eqToHom (congrArg F.obj h)` — to turn a `(pushforward).map`-level
  eqToHom into a base-level one (used in `hib`).

## pushPullMap_comp (NOT ADDED — documented route)
- **Why not added:** no `sorry` is permitted in output and the proof is a materially longer (~150-LOC)
  pentagon+naturality calculation; a half-finished body is uncommittable, so the statement is left as a
  documented comment block (in-file, just before `relativeCechComplexOfNerve`).
- **Verified ingredient:** `Scheme.Modules.pseudofunctor_associativity (f := g.left) (g := h.left) (h := Y₁.hom)`
  typechecks (this is the pentagon that replaces `pseudofunctor_right_unitality`).
- **Route (mirror `pushPullMap_id` one step up):**
  1. pushforward coercions on BOTH sides collapse by the `hpf`-style `hom_ext; intro U; rfl`.
  2. `Adjunction.comp_unit_app` to expand `η_{g.left ≫ h.left}` (composite-adjunction unit).
  3. `pseudofunctor_associativity` to reassociate the three pullback comparisons.
  4. `Adjunction.unit_naturality` to slide the inner unit past the outer pushforward.
  5. `erw` + `reassoc_of%` throughout (defeq-not-syntactic, same as `id`).
- **Next step:** a focused follow-up pass dedicated to `comp`; then assemble
  `pushPullFunctor : (Over X)ᵒᵖ ⥤ X.Modules` from `pushPullObj`/`pushPullMap` + both laws, and discharge
  the `CechNerve` hole (L89) that consumes it.

## Blueprint markers
- `lem:push_pull_functor` (`\lean{pushPullMap_id}`, `\lean{pushPullMap_comp}`): `pushPullMap_id` is now
  formalized + proof-closed (ready for `\leanok` by the deterministic sync). `pushPullMap_comp` is NOT yet
  a declaration (route documented) — the `\lean{pushPullMap_comp}` pin will not resolve until `comp` lands;
  this is the same status as before this iter (it was a comment then too).

## Why I stopped
- **Real progress:** 1 axiom-clean declaration added — `AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_id` (≈ line 216,
  verified `{propext, Classical.choice, Quot.sound}`, full-file build EXIT=0).
- **Partial:** `pushPullMap_comp` is the remaining half of `lem:push_pull_functor`. The blocker is not a
  missing ingredient (the pentagon `pseudofunctor_associativity` is confirmed present and typechecks) but
  the sheer length of the mate calculation; it is left documented rather than half-built (no `sorry`).
- The 3 downstream sorries (`CechAcyclic.affine`, `cech_computes_higherDirectImage`, `cech_flatBaseChange`)
  were not touched — gated on absent Mathlib spectral-sequence / affine-acyclicity / flat-base-change infra
  (out of scope, per objectives).

Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
127	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
128	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
129	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
130	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
131	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
132	
133	### Subagents
134	
135	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
136	
137	### Review Agent
138	
139	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
140	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
141	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
142	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
143	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
144	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
145	
146	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
147	
148	These run automatically between phases:
149	
150	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
151	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
152	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
153

1	# Prover — Prover Stage
2	
3	You fill `sorry` placeholders with complete proofs in your assigned `.lean` file.
4	
5	## Workflow
6	
7	1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
8	2. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts, dead ends, relevant lemmas on your file.
9	3. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments (file-specific user hints).
10	4. **Read the relevant blueprint chapter before writing Lean code.** The chapter holds the mathematical proof sketch you must align with. When stuck, re-reading the blueprint is often the fastest path forward.
11	5. Replace `sorry` with Lean proofs. Push as far as possible.
12	6. **Always save partial progress in the code.** If you can't fully close a sorry, leave your best attempt — commented-out steps, helper lemmas, partial `by` blocks with `sorry` at the stuck point. The file must still compile (use scoped `sorry` if needed), but your work must be visible for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to a bare `sorry` — that wastes the work.
13	7. Write your results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
14	
15	**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.
16	
17	## Protected declarations
18	
19	`archon-protected.yaml` lists declarations with **frozen signatures**. You may fill their proof bodies but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.
20	
21	## Avoid early termination
22	
23	- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
24	- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
25	- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
26	- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
27	- **Decompose**: break the proof into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available: L1, L2, L3, …) and solve each individually until the entire goal closes.
28	
29	## Task completion criteria
30	
31	Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:
32	
33	1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
34	2. Zero axioms introduced.
35	3. The file compiles cleanly.
36	
37	## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof
38	
39	When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, you have one honest move: leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature** so the type itself encodes the claim. You must NOT substitute a tautologically-true type to trivialize the body. Three patterns are banned:
40	
41	- **Reflexive-iso placeholder.** Replacing a non-trivial `X ≅ Y` (or `Nonempty (X ≅ Y)`) target with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`. The type now asserts nothing; the docstring's "iter-N placeholder" disclosure does not undo the structural lie, and the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference will resolve to this hollow declaration.
42	- **`Classical.choice` body around an explicit witness.** Writing `Classical.choice (α := X) ⟨witness⟩` to dissolve `Type` vs `Prop` friction — the witness is unreachable through any unfold, so the def is indistinguishable from `Classical.choice ⟨any-other-X⟩` downstream. Either use `Classical.choose` on the existential chain (preserves the spec), or `Nonempty.some`-with-witness; or ship a typed `sorry`.
43	- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`.** `proof_wanted` discards the declaration post-elaboration — the named decl never enters the environment, so the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference breaks. Use `theorem name : T := by sorry` with the intended signature instead.
44	
45	**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content (Kähler differential module, the explicit iso, …) or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, the body is structurally vacuous — ship the typed `sorry` instead.
46	
47	## When infrastructure is missing
48	
49	Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before giving up:
50	
51	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`): "Prove [goal] without using [missing infrastructure], only Mathlib." Even an imperfect sketch is valuable.
52	2. **Try the alternative** — formalize whatever the informal agent suggests.
53	3. **If you still can't**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` and record in your task result what you tried, why it failed, AND the alternative route you found. "I couldn't prove X, but here's approach Y that might work because Z" is far more useful than "infrastructure missing".
54	
55	When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.
56	
57	**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.
58	
59	## Proof style
60	
61	- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
62	- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
63	- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
64	- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
65	- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is purely display-level annotation. Using `show` where `change` is needed produces a linter warning. Default to `change` when in doubt.
66	
67	## Mathlib tags in PROGRESS.md
68	
69	The plan agent tags suggested lemmas:
70	
71	- `[verified]` — confirmed to exist.
72	- `[expected]` — guessed by naming convention. Quick `lean_local_search`; pivot if it doesn't exist.
73	- `[gap]` — verified NOT in Mathlib. Don't waste search time; formalize a workaround.
74	
75	## LSP MCP tools
76	
77	The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (full names like `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal` / `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`). The lean4 skill reference uses short names (`lean_goal`, `lean_local_search`, …) — same tools, NOT shell binaries.
78	
79	- Always invoke through your tool-call interface, the same way as `Read` or `Grep`.
80	- **Never** call `Bash` with `lean_goal …` etc. — there is no such shell command.
81	- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file. If it returns `success: false` the server is cold; retry once or run `lake build` once via Bash, then retry.
82	
83	## Search protocol
84	
85	Follow `references/lean-lsp-tools-api.md`. Key priority:
86	
87	1. `lean_local_search` first.
88	2. `lean_leansearch` for semantic search — describe the mathematical content, not just the name.
89	3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
90	4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.
91	
92	## Tooling traps
93	
94	- **Trust `goals_after`, not just empty diagnostics**: in `lean_multi_attempt`, `diagnostics: []` can be misleading (line-scoping quirks attach errors to the enclosing `by` block). Check that `goals_after` is empty or has advanced.
95	- **Beware `lean_run_code` with imports**: standalone snippets can silently swallow elaboration errors when they contain imports. Rely on the actual file's `lean_diagnostic_messages` for authoritative verification.
96	
97	## Logging
98	
99	Write to `task_results/<your_file>.md` (mirror your `.lean` path: `Algebra/WLocal.lean` → `task_results/Algebra_WLocal.lean.md`):
100	
101	```markdown
102	# Algebra/WLocal.lean
103	
104	## wLocal_iff (line 45)
105	### Attempt 1
106	- **Approach:** Direct case split on maximal ideals
107	- **Result:** FAILED — needed IsLocalRing instance, not available
108	- **Dead end:** direct case split without IsLocalRing
109	
110	### Attempt 2
111	- **Approach:** Stacks 0A31, characterize via bijection on spectra
112	- **Result:** RESOLVED
113	- **Key insight:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective` bridges the gap
114	
115	## helper_bijective (line 78)
116	### Attempt 1
117	- **Approach:** Split into injectivity + surjectivity; prove injectivity
118	- **Result:** PARTIAL (branch closed) — injectivity closed; surjectivity remains
119	- **Next step:** `PrimeSpectrum.range_comap_of_surjective` for the right branch
120	- **Lemmas found:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_surjective`
121	```
122	
123	**Rules**: one section per theorem/lemma in your file. Each attempt records approach, result (RESOLVED / FAILED / PARTIAL / IN PROGRESS), dead-end warnings or next steps. Log negative search results ("Searched 'projective module infinite rank' — nothing in Mathlib"). The plan agent merges these into `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md` next iter.
124	
125	**Read-only context**: read `task_pending.md` (prior attempts on your file) and `task_done.md` (when the problem resembles a completed one). Do not write to either.
126	
127	## End-of-session handoff
128	
129	Before stopping:
130	
131	1. Write `task_results/<your_file>.md` with: current result, lemmas discovered, concrete next step, dead-end warnings.
132	2. Save all changes; ensure the file compiles (use scoped `sorry` for stuck parts if needed).
133

Check your .lean file for /- USER: ... -/ comments for file-specific hints.

IMPORTANT:
- You own ONLY the file assigned below. Do NOT edit any other .lean file.
- Write your results to /home/archon/FormalizationProjects/Algebraic-Jacobian-Challenge/.archon/task_results/<your_file>.md when done.
- Do NOT edit PROGRESS.md, task_pending.md, or task_done.md.
- Missing Mathlib infrastructure is NEVER a valid reason to leave a sorry.
- NEVER revert to a bare sorry. Always leave your partial proof attempt in the code.

Blueprint chapter for your file: blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_TensorObjSubstrate.tex
- Read it BEFORE writing any Lean code — it contains the informal proof
  written by the plan agent.
- After you formalize a declaration, mark its blueprint environment with \leanok.
- If the chapter file does not yet exist, create it with a minimal \chapter block
  and note in your task_results that the plan agent should flesh it out.
Active prover mode: **fine-grained**

## Your goal

Translate each sentence of the informal proof in the blueprint chapter into an atomic named Lean lemma, then prove each lemma independently. The theorem is never attacked whole. Progress is measured per-sentence, not per-theorem. If sentence themselves can be decomposed further, do so. 

## Why this matters

Provers stall on large goals because the first difficult step blocks the rest. By forcing each sentence to become its own named claim, every step either closes quickly or fails with a precise, fixable error — instead of a vague "I can't prove this big thing."

## Workflow

1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your current objectives (read only — do not edit it).
2. **Read your blueprint chapter sentence by sentence.** For the section corresponding to your assigned theorem, list every individual mathematical claim made in the informal proof (not just top-level lemmas — every "since X", "by Y", "note that Z" is a candidate).
3. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts and dead ends.
4. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments.
5. For each identified sentence:
   a. Introduce a named `private lemma` encoding that sentence's claim.
   b. Immediately attempt to prove it — using LSP tools, Mathlib search, and tactic exploration.
   c. If it closes: mark RESOLVED in your log; move on.
   d. If it does not close within reasonable effort: leave a `sorry` with the partial tactic attempt visible (never revert to bare `sorry`), naming the specific blocker; move on to the next sentence.
6. After all sentences are attempted, assemble the main theorem from the sub-lemma names (the assembly itself may use `sorry` if some sub-lemmas are still open).
7. Verify the file compiles.
8. Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.

**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, other agents' files, or the blueprint chapter.

## Sentence identification heuristics

Read the informal proof text and extract each **atomic mathematical claim** — a sentence that, if true, advances the proof by one logical step. Examples:

- "The map φ is injective" → `lemma phi_injective : Function.Injective φ := …`
- "Since R is local, the maximal ideal is unique" → `lemma unique_maximal_of_local [IsLocalRing R] : … := …`
- "The kernel is contained in the Jacobson radical" → `lemma ker_le_jacobson : … := …`

Skip claims that are definitional (follow immediately from `unfold` or `simp`) — fold those into the first proof that needs them.

## Prove each sentence atomically

For each sub-lemma:

1. State the type precisely — it must exactly encode the blueprint sentence.
2. Try `simp`, `ring`, `omega`, `norm_num`, `exact?`, `apply?` as quick checks.
3. Use `lean_leansearch` with the mathematical content as the query.
4. If a Mathlib lemma covers it exactly: `exact MathLibLemmaName` or `apply`.
5. If it requires a short tactic proof: write it.
6. If it requires significant work: write whatever partial proof you have, then leave a scoped `sorry` at the stuck point. Do NOT revert to a bare `sorry` — keep the partial attempt visible.

**You are fully authorized to make every proof decision within your assigned file without waiting for the planner.** "I'll let the planner decide" is never valid for a tactic or approach choice — make the call, attempt it, document the outcome.

**Time budget**: spend at most a few tool calls per sentence before declaring PARTIAL and moving on. The point of this mode is coverage across all sentences, not depth on any single one.

**Comments are not progress. Code is.** If you write `-- TODO: try simp [X]` or `/- This could work via Y -/`, you must attempt it before writing the comment. Only leave a comment about an approach if you tried it and it failed with a named error.

## Protected declarations

Read `archon-protected.yaml` before touching any declaration. Do not change protected signatures.

## Completion criteria

This mode succeeds when:

- Every informal sentence has a corresponding named sub-lemma (proved or `sorry`).
- The main theorem's proof body references the sub-lemma names.
- The file compiles.

A subsequent `prove` pass targeting specific open sub-lemmas is expected.

## LSP MCP tools

The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (`mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal`, `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`, etc.). Never call them as shell commands.

- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file.
- Use `lean_goal` after each tactic to confirm progress.

## Search protocol

1. `lean_local_search` first.
2. `lean_leansearch` — describe the mathematical content of the sentence.
3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.

## Logging

```markdown
# Algebra/WLocal.lean

## wLocal_iff — fine-grained pass

### Sentence 1: "The map φ is injective" → `phi_injective`
- **Result:** RESOLVED — `exact PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective`

### Sentence 2: "The kernel is contained in the Jacobson radical" → `ker_le_jacobson`
- **Result:** PARTIAL — started from `Ideal.mem_jacobson_iff`, stuck at membership criterion
- **Dead end:** `exact?` found nothing; `Ideal.jacobson_mono` applies but needs surjectivity first
- **Next step:** prove surjectivity (sentence 4) first, then return here

### Sentence 3: "Since R is local, the maximal ideal is unique" → `unique_maximal_of_local`
- **Result:** RESOLVED — direct from `IsLocalRing.eq_maximalIdeal`

### Assembly (wLocal_iff)
- References: `phi_injective`, `ker_le_jacobson` (sorry), `unique_maximal_of_local`
- Compiles with scoped sorry on `ker_le_jacobson`.

## Summary
- 2/3 sentences closed; 1 blocked (kernel ⊆ Jacobson).
- Recommended next: `prove` targeting `ker_le_jacobson` with directive to establish surjectivity first.
```

## End-of-session handoff

**Before declaring done, run this self-review:**

1. Did I attempt every approach I wrote in a comment or TODO? If not, go back and attempt them.
2. After all sentences, did I try to fully assemble the main theorem? Even with sorry sub-lemmas, a structured assembly often reveals which sub-lemmas are actually easy.
3. Are there open sub-lemmas I judged "too hard" without a genuine attempt? Spend one more round on each.

Before writing results:

1. Verify the file compiles.
2. Write `task_results/<your_file>.md` with one entry per identified sentence: name, type (brief), result (RESOLVED / PARTIAL / sorry with blocker).
3. **Write a `## Summary` section**: N/M sentences closed, sorry count before → after, which sentences are open and exactly why.
4. **Write a `## Why I stopped` section** — be honest; only closed sub-lemma proofs count as progress. Sub-lemmas extracted with only `sorry` bodies and no proof attempt are cosmetics:
   - `Real progress`: M/N sentences closed — list which ones and sorry count before → after.
   - `Partial progress`: measurable code advance (partial proof bodies, one branch closed); list open sentences with their specific blockers (not "it's hard" — the exact type mismatch or missing ingredient).
   - `Approaches written but not attempted`: identified routes in comments or log but did not attempt — name each. Planner will re-assign with directive to attempt them.
   - `Blueprint sentences not identifiable`: the informal proof is too vague to extract atomic sentences — describe what's missing and what the blueprint needs.
   - `All sentences already proved`: file had no sorry to decompose.
   - `Directive not followed`: explain the deviation.
